coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of ternary Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 heterostructure with following Type II and Z-Scheme dual pathways for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Shi, Weilong
Liu, Chang
Li, Mingyang
Lin, Xue
Guo, Feng
Shi, Junyou
coredata.dc:description: A novel ternary Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 (APO/CPO/CN) heterostructure photocatalyst was successfully synthesized via a simple precipitation method for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. The experimental result reveals that the ternary APO/CPO/CN heterojunction showed enhanced photocatalytic performance compared with single semiconductor CPO and CN, binary composite CPO/CN. And APO/CPO/CN-15 % composite exhibits highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency, which can degrade TC around 88 % under visible light within 120 min. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is due to the synergy effects between CPO, CN and APO with the aid of following Z-scheme and Type II heterojunction dual pathways for effective separation of photogenerated charges. This work provides a new approach in the rational design of ternary heterojunction photocatalyst with multilevel electron transfer for environmental decontamination.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of ternary Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 heterostructure with following Type II and Z-Scheme dual pathways for enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ternary Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 (APO/CPO/CN) heterostructure photocatalyst was successfully synthesized via a simple precipitation method for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light irradiation. The experimental result reveals that the ternary APO/CPO/CN heterojunction showed enhanced photocatalytic performance compared with single semiconductor CPO and CN, binary composite CPO/CN. And APO/CPO/CN-15 % composite exhibits highest photocatalytic degradation efficiency, which can degrade TC around 88 % under visible light within 120 min. The enhanced photocatalytic performance is due to the synergy effects between CPO, CN and APO with the aid of following Z-scheme and Type II heterojunction dual pathways for effective separation of photogenerated charges. This work provides a new approach in the rational design of ternary heterojunction photocatalyst with multilevel electron transfer for environmental decontamination.

ce:section.ce:para: Heterogeneous photocatalysis is considered as one of the most promising technologies to degrade organic pollutants in the aqueous solution by the utilization of solar energy owing to its simplicity, cost-efficiency and non-pollution (Ye et al., 2013; Qi et al., 2017; Ong et al., 2018; Guo et al., 2019a; Wang et al., 2020, 2017; Wang et al., 2018a). Since the previous report by Honda and Fujishima in 1972, various of photocatalysts have been developed in the photocatalytic applications (Fujishima and Honda, 1972; Lin et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019a, b; Lu et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2020). However, there still exists some disadvantages for the most of single photocatalysts, such as a wide bandgap, fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and poor visible-light absorption, which blocks their practical applications (Perreault et al., 2015; Meng et al., 2017; Liao et al., 2014; Chen et al., 2017a; Guo et al., 2019b; Che et al., 2020; Shi et al., 2019a).

Generally, the charge migration pathway of heterostructure photocatalysts follows either type II heterojunction or Z-scheme approach that can enhance the rapid separation of photogenerated carriers, which is considered to be an effective method to improve the photocatalytic performance of single semiconductor materials (Chen et al., 2017a; Zhu et al., 2017; Li et al., 2015; Guo et al., 2019c). Up to now, many binary type II or Z-scheme heterostructure photocatalysts have been reported toward the organic pollution degradation under visible light irradiation, such as ZnIn2S4/g-C3N4 (Guo et al., 2017), CuInS2/g-C3N4 (Guo et al., 2019c), BiVO4/g-C3N4 (Guo et al., 2014), InVO4/g-C3N4 (Shi et al., 2014), etc. Recent years, ternary heterojunctions have also been investigated in order to further improve the photocatalytic activity. For example, Yang et al. synthesized flower-like CdWO4/BiOCl/Bi2WO6 ternary hybrid via one-pot hydrothermal process and showed outstanding photocatalytic performance for the degradation of RhB in comparison of binary CdWO4/Bi2WO6 composite (Yang et al., 2018). Di et al. used ZnO/g-C3N4/Fe2O3 as a photocatalyst for efficiently photodegrading spiked sulfonamides in actual surface water matrix, which is higher than single g-C3N4 and binary ZnO/g-C3N4 (Di et al., 2019).

Although the designed ternary type-II heterojunction can further efficiently enhance the separation of photo-induced charges, the strong redox ability of the photocatalyst is sacrificed due to the lower reduction and oxidation processes occurrence. It is generally known that electrons accumulate on the conduction band of photocatalyst semiconductor I with the higher reduction potential, and holes leave on the valance band of photocatalyst semiconductor II with the higher oxidation potential, thus not only effectively separating the electron–hole pairs, but also achieving an optimal redox ability. Therefore, it is advisable to construct a dual-path (Z-Scheme and Type II) ternary heterostructure photocatalyst. Due to the fact that the CB and VB of Co3(PO4)2 are given (CB = −0.73 eV vs. NHE and VB =1.62 eV vs. NHE), and the CB and VB of g-C3N4 are −1.12 eV vs. NHE and 1.57 eV vs. NHE, respectively (Samal et al., 2016). Consequently, Co3(PO4)2 and g-C3N4 are more likely to form type II heterojunction in theory for enhancing the photocatalytic activity. To further accelerate charge separation of Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 type II heterojunction and keep the higher redox potentials, the combination of an efficient Z-Scheme photocatalytic system is imperative. More recently, coupling Ag3PO4 with suitable band-gap semiconductors to construct Z-scheme heterostructure photocatalysts, which by-product Ag nanoparticles in the photocatalysis process can be used as a charge transmission bridge, has been proved to be an effective method to separate photo-generated electron-hole pairs and prevent the reductive decomposition of Ag3PO4, such as Ag3PO4/Ag/SiC (Chen et al., 2015), Ag3PO4/Ag/CuBi2O4 (Shi et al., 2017), Ag3PO4/Ag/AgI (Cai et al., 2019), Ag3PO4/Ag/MoS2 (Wang et al., 2018b), etc.

In this work, ternary Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 heterostructure were prepared by a simple precipitation method. The visible-light photocatalytic degradation performances of tetracycline (TC) over as-prepared products were investigated. A battery of characterizations were carried out to explore the reasons for the improvement of the activity of the composite photocatalyst. The electron transfer mechanism of enhanced photocatalytic performance of Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 was also discussed in detail.

G-C3N4 was prepared by directly heating melamine to 550 ℃ at a heating rate of 2 ℃/min, and kept at this temperature for another 4 h in air. Then cooling it to room temperature at the same rate. The obtained yellow powder was collected and named as CN.

The prepared g-C3N4 (0.2 g) and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.235 g) were mixed with 50 mL of deionized water by stirred magnetically for 2 h. Then, an aqueous solution of (NH4)2HPO4 (0.07 g (NH4)2HPO4, 50 mL H2O) was dropped slowly into the above solution, which was stirred for another 2 h. The resulting product was collected, washed with ethanol and distilled water several times, and then dried at 70℃ for 12 h. The obtained Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 powder was denoted as CPO/CN, and pure Co3(PO4)2 (CPO) was prepared with similar method without introducing g-C3N4.

The prepared CPO/CN (0.2 g) was dissolved into 50 mL of water, and then different volumes of AgNO3 (0.03 M) and Na3PO4·12H2O (0.01 M) aqueous solution were mixed into above solution in order and stirred at room temperature for 3 h. The resulting suspension was washed with deionized water three times and dried at 70 ℃ for 12 h. The theoretical weight ratios of Ag3PO4 in Ag3PO4/Co3(PO4)2/g-C3N4 composites were 5 %, 10 %, 15 %, 20 % and 25 %, and marked as APO/CPO/CN-5 %, APO/CPO/CN-10 %, APO/CPO/CN-15 %, APO/CPO/CN-20 %, APO/CPO/CN-25 %, respectively. The pristine Ag3PO4 (APO) was prepared with similar method without introducing CPO/CN.
The characterization methods and the procedure of photocatalytic activity studies and actives capturing experiments are listed in the Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 a shows the XRD diagrams of APO, CN, CPO, CPO/CN, APO/CPO/CN-5 %, APO/CPO/CN-10 %, APO/CPO/CN-15 %, APO/CPO/CN-20 %, and APO/CPO/CN-25 %, respectively. All the diffraction peaks of CPO can be matched to specific crystal planes of the CPO (JCPDS No. 41-0375) (Geng et al., 2019). For the pure CN sample, the diffraction peak at 27.4° can be clearly observed, which are indexed to (002) plane of g-C3N4 (Kumar et al., 2013). The XRD diffraction peaks of APO are obviously assigned to the crystal planes of APO (JCPDS No. 06-0505) (Miao et al., 2018a). The diffraction peaks of pure samples can be found in all binary and ternary composites, and the intensities of diffraction peaks of APO increase with increasing weight ratio of APO in the ternary composites. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra of CN, CPO, CPO/CN, APO/CPO/CN-5 %, APO/CPO/CN-10 %, APO/CPO/CN-15 %, APO/CPO/CN-20 %, and APO/CPO/CN-25 % samples are given in Fig. 1b. In the FT-IR spectrum, the peak appears at about 808 cm−1, which is attributed to the characteristic breathing mode of tri-s-triazine units in CN (Xiao et al., 2018). Moreover, the peaks in the 1200−1640 cm−1 region can be ascribed to the stretching vibration modes of CN heterocycle in CN (Safaei et al., 2018). The wide peak near 3250 cm-1 is related to the tensile mode of NH group at the defect site of aromatic ring (Patnaik et al., 2018). Another peak at about 3430 cm−1 attributes to the OH stretching deformation (Fernando et al., 2015). Notably, after introduction of APO, a new peak appears near 1010 cm−1, which is assigned to the PO stretching vibration (Yang et al., 2013). The results of XRD and FT-IR confirm the successful formation of APO/CPO/CN ternary composite.

The SEM images of as-prepared APO, CPO, CN and APO/CPO/CN-15 % are presented in Fig. 2 a–d. It is found that the APO particles shows the regular spheres with 200−300 nm (Fig. 2a). The CPO sample exhibits the archetypical 3D flower-like structures with the thicknesses of each nanosheet is about 40−100 nm (Fig. 2b). The CN sample displays irregular lamellar structure which are stacked together (Fig. 2c). As presented in Fig. 2d, APO/CPO/CN-15 % shows an irregular shape with aggregations of many nanosheets, while it is hardly observed flower-like CPO due to the introduction of CN nanosheets. In addition, APO nanoparticles were loaded on the lamellar structure CPO/CN.

In order to observe the samples we synthesized more clearly, TEM images of as-prepared APO/CPO/CN-15 % sample were taken and displayed in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3a, it can be seen that the APO/CPO/CN-15 % was composed of CPO nanosheets and CN nanosheets. HRTEM image was carried out to further confirm the formation of the ternary heterostructure (Fig. 3b). Significantly, a number of APO nanoparticles can be found on the surface of sheet-on-sheet structured CPO/CN. By measuring the lattice fringes, interplanar spacing are around 0.239 and 0.244 nm, which correspond to the (211) plane of APO and the (031) plane of CPO, respectively (Guo et al., 2018; Li et al., 2014). And the blurry region below the blue dash line correspond to CN due to its poor crystallinity. To further study the composition of the APO/CPO/CN nanocomposite, HAADF-STEM and elemental scanning analysis were carried out. According to the STEM image (Fig. S1a), the EDX mappings of Ag, Co, P, O, C and N elements in the region are detected as exhibited in Fig. S1b-h. The above results provide solid evidence that APO/CPO/CN nanocomposite was successfully synthesized.

XPS was employed to analyze the surface chemical composition of as-prepared photocatalysts. Fig. S2 provides the XPS full spectrum of APO/CPO/CN-15 %. Only C 1s, N 1s, Ag 3d, Co 2p, P 2p and O 1s are detected. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4 a) clearly shows three characteristic peaks located at 284.6, 285.3 and 288.3 eV, respectively, corresponding to the adsorbed carbon, CN and CN groups, respectively (Yu et al., 2013). The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) is separated at three binding energies at 398.2, 399.1 and 400.6 eV, which are associated with triazine rings (CNC), tertiary nitrogen (N(C)3) and CN(H)2, respectively (Wang et al., 2016). The Ag 3d spectrum (Fig. 4c) shows two major peaks located at 367.8 (Ag 3d5/2) and 373.8 eV (Ag 3d3/2), which can be attributed to Ag+ species (Miao et al., 2018b). In Fig. 4d, the Co 2p spectrum displays two typical peaks located at 780.1 and 796.2 eV with another two satellite peaks at 786.0 eV and 802.7 eV, proving that Co exists as Co2+ in CPO (Li et al., 2013). The P 2p spectrum (Fig. 4e) displays two peaks, the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 peaks were observed at 133.4 and 134.4 eV binding energies and O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 4f) shows two strong peaks at about 531.3 and 532.1 eV, which are attributed to the phosphate groups (Tang et al., 2017).

The optical properties of as-synthetized products were tested by the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS). Fig. 5 presents the UV–vis DRS of APO, CN, CPO, CPO/CN and APO/CPO/CN-15 %. As can be seen, the light absorption edge of CN is about 470 nm, which is in agreement with previous report (Ma et al., 2016). Evidently, both the pure CPO and APO shows the intense absorption bands in the visible-light region. Moreover, after coupling with CPO, the CPO/CN exhibits obvious enhanced visible-light absorption compared with pure CN. Furthermore, it is obtained that the APO/CPO/CN ternary nanocomposite displays higher absorption ability compared the CPO/CN.

The band gaps (Eg ) can be calculated by Tauc equation (Lv et al., 2017) as following: αhν = A[hν-Eg]n/2 where α, h, ν and A is absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency and a constant, respectively. The value of n depends on the type of optical transition of the semiconductor (n = 1 for direct transition and n = 4 for indirect transition). For APO, CPO and CN, the n value equal to 1, respectively (Cai et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2019b; Dong et al., 2014). As shown in Fig. 6 a, the E g of APO, CPO and CN are estimated to be 2.31, 2.35 and 2.73 eV, respectively. In order to determine the absolute band-edge positions of as-prepared, electrochemical analysis was carried out to measure their flat band potentials. Fig. 6b–d display the Mott-Schottky diagrams of APO, CPO and CN, that is, C−2 as a function of electrochemical potentials, where the capacitance C is obtained from the impedance measurement. The results indicate that the flat-band potentials of APO (0.46 V vs. NHE), CPO (-0.54 V vs. NHE) and CN (-1.05 V vs. NHE) can be obtained at the same frequency. The results also show that APO, CPO and CN are n-type semiconductors with the positive slopes of the linear plots (You et al., 2018). In general, in the n-type semiconductor, the flat-band level is 0.1 to 0.3 eV higher than the potential of the conduction band (CB) (Meng et al., 2013). Thus, it is estimated that the CB potentials of APO, CPO and CN are 0.26, -0.74 and -1.25 eV, respectively. According to their band gaps of the three photocatalysts, the VB potentials for APO, CPO and CN were calculated to be 2.57, 1.61 and 1.48 eV, respectively.

The visible-light photocatalytic activity of the as-prepared photocatalysts was investigated by photocatalytic degradation of TC (Fig. 7 a). Before photocatalysis, the solution of TC and as-prepared photocatalysts were mixed and magnetically stirred in dark for 30 min to reaches the adsorption equilibrium. After 30 min dark adsorption, all the samples were exposed under the visible light irradiation to participate in the photocatalytic reaction. During the photocatalytic reaction, the decrease of TC concentration were very poor after 120 min of visible-light irradiation for pure CPO and CN samples. This may be due to the easy recombination of photoelectric charge and poor visible light utilization over single phase semiconductors. Compared with the pure phase samples (CPO and CN), the CPO/CN composite samples exhibited remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity (48 % degradation within 120 min), which is mainly ascribed to the formation of heterojunction between CPO and CN. Notably, after coating APO nanoparticles, the photocatalytic performance of CPO/CN composite has been further improved, and APO/CPO/CN-15 % sample showed the highest efficiency among all the ternary composites, which the concentration of TC is reduced by around 88 %. The temporal evolution of HPLC chromatograms changes during the photodegradation process of TC by APO/CPO/CN-15 % is displayed in Fig. S3. In addition, we further compared the degradation of TC by g-C3N4-based heterojunction photocatalysts reported in recent years. As depicted in Table 1 , although it is difficult to compare directly because of the different photocatalytic reaction parameters, the photocatalytic degradation rate of APO/CPO/CN-15 % is still higher than that of other photocatalysts, which indicates that APO/CPO/CN heterojunction can be used as a high-efficient photocatalyst to remove TC from solution. Meanwhile, the degradation rate constant k (min−1) for the photodegradation of TC over all photocatalysts, the k values were also summarized in Fig. 7b. The k value of APO/CPO/CN-15 % (0.0159 min−1) are about 3.38, 5.88 and 9.93 times in comparison with that of CPO/CN (0.0047 min−1), CPO (0.0027 min−1) and CN (0.0016 min−1), respectively. Moreover, to further test the degree of TC mineralization during the photocatalytic process, the removal rate of TOC of as-prepared composite was investigated. As presented in Fig. S4, it is found that barely 0.5 % TOC was reduced in the absence of photocatalyst, while 56.6 % of TOC removal rate was decreased using APO/CPO/CN-15 % composite, indicating the APO/CPO/CN-15 % composites have effective mineralization efficiency for TC photodegradation.

To evaluate the stability of the APO/CPO/CN-15 % sample, the additional five-time cycling experiments have been carried out to degrade TC under visible light (Fig. 8 a). After five times of the photodegradation of TC, the high photocatalytic activity of APO/CPO/CN was effectively maintained except for 6.8 % decrease in degradation efficiency, suggesting that APO/CPO/CN possess high stability under visible light. Fig. 8b shows the XRD patterns of the APO/CPO/CN-15 % sample before and after the photocatalytic reaction. After photocatalytic reaction, the peaks of APO/CPO/CN-15 % sample were almost consistent with that of fresh photocatalyst, which further proves the comparative stability of APO/CPO/CN-15 % composite.

The BET surface areas of as-prepared samples were analyzed by the automatic surface area analyzer. As shown in Fig. 9 , all the as-fabricated samples exhibit type-IV isotherms, indicating that the photocatalysts displayed mesoporous structure. Among them, the calculated BET surface area of APO/CPO/CN-15 % is 16.64 m2 g−1, which is larger than that of single or binary samples. The enlarged BET surface area of APO/CPO/CN-15 % is beneficial for improving the photocatalytic activity.

In order to explore the reasons for the improvement of the activity of the composite catalyst, a series of characterizations were carried out. The PL spectra of CN, CPO/CN and APO/CPO/CN-15 % excited at 325 nm were illustrated in Fig. 10 a. Compared with CN and CPO/CN, the intensities of the peaks for APO/CPO/CN-15 % were much lower, demonstrating that the ternary nanocomposite have a lower recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Shi et al., 2019c, d). Time-resolved PL spectroscopy was recorded to investigate the decay lifetime of photo-generated carriers of CN, CPO/CN and APO/CPO/CN-15 % samples and displayed in Fig. 10b. The average decay lifetime of photo-generated carriers over the irradiated APO/CPO/CN-15 % was 1.16 ns, much lower than those in CN (1.44 ns) and CPO/CN (1.23 ns). The fluorescence lifetime of APO/CPO/CN-15 % was evidently shortened, which further confirms that there was a faster electron transfer in the ternary nanocomposite (Lee et al., 2011). Fig. 10c shows the photocurrent responses versus time with on-off cycles of the irradiation signal over as-synthesized products. As expected, the ternary composite shows the highest photocurrent intensity, suggesting the excellent migration efficiency of photoexcited charge carriers, which is consistent with the results of PL. Furthermore, the charge transport behavior in the synthetized photocatalysts is explored by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and is displayed in Fig. 10d. It can be seen that the smallest charge transfer resistance can be observed for APO/CPO/CN-15 %, indicating that ternary nanocomposite exhibits fastest transfer rate of photo-induced carriers among the samples (Shi et al., 2019e; Liu et al., 2018).

Fig. 11 exhibits the photodegradation of TC by the APO/CPO/CN photocatalyst after the addition of 1 mmol various scavengers (disodium ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid, EDTA, ascorbic acid, VC and 2-propanol, IPA), which were used as the hole (h+) scavenger, superoxide radical (
O2 −) scavenger and hydroxyl radical (
OH) scavenger, respectively. As can be seen, the degradation rate decreases obviously in the presence of VC, IPA and EDTA, it is clear that
O2 −,
OH and h+ play the major reactive species during the photocatalytic process in this study.

To further clarify the degradation mechanism, the ESR measurements of the ternary APO/CPO/CN nanocomposite photocatalyst were carried out. As shown in Fig. 12 a and b, no signal peaks of DMPO-
OH and DMPO-
O2 − were found in the dark in the presence of photocatalyst, proving that no
OH/
O2 − radicals were generated in the dark condition. Significantly, the characteristic peaks of the DMPO-
OH/
O2 − can be observed over APO/CPO/CN when exposed to visible light irradiation according to previous reports (Deng et al., 2018; Niu et al., 2013). The above results indicate that
OH and
O2- played key roles in the photocatalysis processes, which is agreement with the data of trapping experiments of active species.

Based on the above analysis, the photocatalytic mechanism of APO/CPO/CN composite was tentatively proposed (Fig. 13 ). Based on the matchable band gap structures of APO, CPO and CN, the separation processes of photoexcited electron-hole pairs can be deduced the following three possible paths (see Fig. 13a–c). With respect to the double type-II charge transfer path of APO/CPO/CN photocatalyst (Fig. 13a), the photogenerated electrons in the CB of CN will firstly migrate to the CB of CPO, then to CB of APO. And the photogenerated holes in the VB of APO will firstly transfer to the VB of CPO then to VB of CN. However, the accumulated photogenerated electrons on CB of APO cannot react with O2 to yield
O2 − due to the more negative standard redox potential of O2/
O2 − (-0.33 eV vs. NHE) than the that of APO (0.26 eV vs. NHE), which is not accordance with the result of the trapping experiments of active species. In Fig. 13b, if APO/CPO/CN heterojunction followed double Z-scheme path, it illustrated that the photogenerated electrons on the CBs of CPO and APO would recombine with the photogenerated holes on the VBs of CN and CPO, respectively. Leaving the photogenerated electrons on the CB of CN and photogenerated holes on the VB of APO produce
O2 − and
OH radicals to participate in photocatalytic degradation reaction, indicating
O2 − and
OH radicals are the main active species, and the participation of h + in the reaction is less obvious. This hypothesis is also not consistent with the result of active species trapping (Fig. 11). Therefore, the electron transfer process of the APO/CPO/CN composite may be two different kinds of paths. That is, type II and Z-scheme dual paths are favorable for the photocatalytic mechanism of APO/CPO/CN composite (as shown in Fig. 13c). Under the illumination of visible light, APO, CPO and CN can be excited to generate electron-hole pairs. The edge potentials of CB bottom and VB top for APO, CPO and CN can be observed (the ECB is 0.26 eV, -0.74 eV and -1.25 eV vs. NHE, and the EVB is 2.57 eV, 1.61 eV and 1.48 eV vs. NHE, respectively). The possible way of carriers migration can be speculated, the e- migrate from the CB of CN to the CB of CPO, while h + remain on the VB of CN due to the formation of CPO/CN type II heterojunction. Then, the e- on the CB of APO tend to recombine with the h+ on the VB of CPO with the aid of Z-scheme heterojunction. Furthermore, the CB edge potential of CPO (-0.74 eV vs. NHE) is more negative than the standard redox potential O2/
O2- (0.33 eV vs. NHE), resulting in a certain ability of eCB- to produce
O2-. Since the EVB of APO (2.57 eV vs. NHE) is more positive than the potential
OH/OH- (2.40 eV vs. NHE), the h+ on VB of APO could oxidize OH- to obtain
OH. The active species with strong oxidizing properties, including
O2- and
OH can degrade TC. Finally, the h+ in the VB of CN with strong oxidation ability can directly degrade TC. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation of TC by APO/CPO/CN can be described as follows:
(1) APO/CPO/CN + hv → APO/CPO/CN (h++e−)
(2) O2 + e− →
O2 −
(3) h+ + OH− →
OH
(4) TC +
O2 −, h+,
OH →degradation products (H2O, CO2, etc.)

The intermediates formed by the photocatalytic oxidation process of TC were tested by LCMS and analyzed according to previous works (Lu et al., 2018). From Fig. S5, it can be inferred that under the attack of the main reaction groups (h+ ,
O2 − and
OH), TC molecules are decomposed into low molecular weight molecular organic compounds with m/z values of 475, 438, 400, 274, 233, 210 and 149 through a series of reactions, such as dehydroxylation, deamination and ring opening. Finally, CO2 and H2O are formed by the photodegradation process, and the possible degradation pathways are exhibited in Fig. 14 .

In summary, a novel APO/CPO/CN photocatalyst was successfully fabricated by anchoring APO nanoparticles on the CPO and CN with a simple precipitation method. Compared with single- and binary- component system, such as CPO, CN and CPO/CN, the as-prepared ternary APO/CPO/CN composite showed improved photocatalytic performance for degradation of TC under visible light. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of ternary nanocomposite is mainly ascribed to the combination of Z-Scheme and Type II heterojunction dual electron transfer pathways, which effectively improves the optical absorption and separation efficiency of electron hole pairs. The present work may provide a new approach in the rational design of ternary heterojunction photocatalyst with multilevel electron transfer for environmental decontamination.
